name: Tailwind CSS
what_it_does: Utility-first CSS framework with JIT compilation that generates only the CSS classes used in your project, enabling rapid UI development via composable utility classes in HTML/JSX.
use_cases: Style a React or Vue component by applying utility classes (e.g., 'flex items-center gap-4 p-6 rounded-lg') directly in JSX/HTML without writing custom CSS
Configure tailwind.config.js content paths so the JIT engine scans template files and purges unused classes for a minimal production CSS bundle
Use arbitrary value syntax (e.g., 'w-[328px]', 'text-[#ff6b35]') to apply one-off design values without leaving the utility-class paradigm
Apply Tailwind utilities within a CSS component class using @apply to keep markup clean while retaining the utility system's consistency
Customize the design system by extending theme.colors, theme.spacing, or theme.fontFamily in tailwind.config.js to match brand tokens
not_for: Projects without a build step — Tailwind v3 JIT and v4 require PostCSS or a CLI build process; it cannot be used as a drop-in CDN stylesheet for production without significant limitations
Teams that prefer semantic, component-scoped CSS — Tailwind's utility-first approach conflicts with BEM or CSS Modules philosophies and requires buy-in across the team
Environments where Tailwind's large initial class vocabulary makes AI-generated code review difficult without familiarity with the utility naming system
best_when: Building UI with a component framework (React, Vue, Svelte) where rapid iteration and design-system consistency matter more than semantic CSS authoring.
avoid_when: Your project has no build pipeline, your team prefers semantic CSS conventions, or you cannot configure the 'content' paths needed for JIT purging.

auth.notes: Local CSS framework — no authentication needed.

pricing.paid_tiers: None (Tailwind UI component library is a separate paid product)
pricing.notes: Core framework is open source. Tailwind UI (pre-built component templates) is a separate paid product.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Tailwind itself produces few runtime errors; misconfigured content paths cause silent class purging with no warning, making missing styles hard to diagnose.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: CSS generation is deterministic given the same content paths, config, and Tailwind version.
known_agent_gotchas: The 'content' array in tailwind.config.js must cover all template file paths — dynamically constructed class names (e.g., string concatenation) will be purged because the JIT scanner is regex-based and cannot evaluate runtime values

Tailwind v3 and v4 have significantly different configuration models — v4 moves config into CSS with @theme blocks rather than a JS config file; agents must verify which version is installed before generating config
Using @apply with utility classes that reference theme values (e.g., '@apply text-primary') requires those values to be defined in tailwind.config.js theme; missing values silently produce nothing
Dark mode must be explicitly enabled in config (darkMode: 'class' or 'media'); without this, dark: variant classes are purged and produce no output
Arbitrary value classes with special characters (e.g., 'bg-[url(\'...\')]') can break if the content scanner regex fails to match the full class string in JSX or template literals

security_score_components.security_notes: Runs locally as a PostCSS plugin or CLI. No network calls. Agents should avoid embedding sensitive values (API keys, secrets) in Tailwind class names or theme config values.
